section.title: Pentecost and the First Church
section.sectionRationale: This is the birthday of the church. The promised Spirit is poured out with wind and fire, the disciples declare the wonders of God in many languages, and the gathered nations hear. Peter interprets it all by Joel and the Psalms, proclaims the crucified and risen Jesus as Lord and Christ, and calls the crowd to repent. Three thousand believe, and the first church is formed, devoted to teaching, fellowship, bread, and prayer.
section.thesis: The exalted Christ pours out the promised Spirit, so that the gospel is proclaimed to every nation, the crucified Jesus is declared Lord and Christ, and a new Spirit-filled community is born that repents, believes, and shares its life together.
section.frameNote: Five movements: the coming of the Spirit with wind, fire, and tongues; the bewildered crowd from every nation; Peter's sermon from Joel and the Psalms proclaiming Jesus as Lord and Christ; the cut-to-the-heart response and three thousand baptized; and the shared life of the first church.

verses.text: When the day of Pentecost came, they were all together in one place.
Suddenly a sound like a mighty rushing wind came from heaven and filled the whole house where they were sitting.
They saw tongues like flames of fire that separated and came to rest on each of them.
And they were all filled with the Holy {{pneuma|Spirit}} and began to speak in other {{glossa|tongues}} as the Spirit enabled them.

units.label: The nations hear in their own languages
units.note: each one heard them in his own tongue

verses.text: Now there were dwelling in Jerusalem God-fearing Jews from every nation under heaven.
And when this sound rang out, a crowd came together in bewilderment, because each one heard them speaking his own language.
Astounded and amazed, they asked, “Are not all these who are speaking Galileans?
How is it then that each of us hears them in his own native language?
Parthians, Medes, and Elamites; residents of Mesopotamia, Judea and Cappadocia, Pontus and Asia,
Phrygia and Pamphylia, Egypt and the parts of Libya near Cyrene; visitors from Rome,
both Jews and converts to Judaism; Cretans and Arabs, we hear them declaring the wonders of God in our own tongues!”
Astounded and perplexed, they asked one another, “What does this mean?”
But others mocked them and said, “They are drunk on new wine!”

units.label: Peter proclaims the risen and exalted Christ
units.note: God has made this Jesus both Lord and Christ

verses.text: Then Peter stood up with the Eleven, lifted up his voice, and addressed the crowd: “Men of Judea and all who dwell in Jerusalem, let this be known to you, and listen carefully to my words.
These men are not drunk, as you suppose. It is only the third hour of the day!
No, this is what was spoken by the prophet Joel:
‘In the last days, God says, I will pour out My Spirit on all people. Your sons and daughters will prophesy, your young men will see visions, your old men will dream dreams.
Even on My menservants and maidservants I will pour out My Spirit in those days, and they will prophesy.
I will show wonders in the heavens above and signs on the earth below, blood and fire and billows of smoke.
The sun will be turned to darkness, and the moon to blood, before the coming of the great and glorious Day of the Lord.
And everyone who calls on the name of the Lord will be saved.’
Men of Israel, listen to this message: Jesus of Nazareth was a man certified by God to you by miracles, wonders, and signs, which God did among you through Him, as you yourselves know.
He was delivered up by God’s set plan and foreknowledge, and you, by the hands of the lawless, put Him to death by nailing Him to the cross.
But God raised Him from the dead, releasing Him from the agony of death, because it was impossible for death to keep Him in its grip.
David says about Him: ‘I saw the Lord always before me; because He is at my right hand, I will not be shaken.
Therefore my heart is glad and my tongue rejoices; my body also will dwell in hope,
because You will not abandon my soul to Hades, nor will You let Your Holy One see decay.
You have made known to me the paths of life; You will fill me with joy in Your presence.’
Brothers, I can tell you with confidence that the patriarch David died and was buried, and his tomb is with us to this day.
But he was a prophet and knew that God had promised him on oath that He would place one of his descendants on his throne.
Foreseeing this, David spoke about the resurrection of the Christ, that He was not abandoned to Hades, nor did His body see decay.
God has raised this Jesus to life, to which we are all witnesses.
Exalted, then, to the right hand of God, He has received from the Father the promised Holy Spirit and has poured out what you now see and hear.
For David did not ascend into heaven, but he himself says: ‘The Lord said to my Lord, “Sit at My right hand
until I make Your enemies a footstool for Your feet.”’
Therefore let all Israel know with certainty that God has made this Jesus, whom you crucified, both Lord and {{christos|Christ}}!”

units.label: Cut to the heart: three thousand believe

verses.text: When the people heard this, they were cut to the heart and asked Peter and the other apostles, “Brothers, what shall we do?”
Peter replied, “{{metanoeo|Repent}} and be baptized, every one of you, in the name of Jesus Christ for the forgiveness of your sins, and you will receive the gift of the Holy Spirit.
This promise belongs to you and your children and to all who are far off, to all whom the Lord our God will call to Himself.”
With many other words he testified, and he urged them, “Be saved from this corrupt generation.”
Those who embraced his message were baptized, and about three thousand were added to the believers that day.

units.label: The shared life of the first church
units.note: devoted to teaching, fellowship, bread, and prayer

verses.text: They devoted themselves to the apostles’ teaching and to the {{koinonia|fellowship}}, to the breaking of bread and to prayer.
A sense of awe came over everyone, and the apostles performed many wonders and signs.
All the believers were together and had everything in common.
Selling their possessions and goods, they shared with anyone who was in need.
With one accord they continued to meet daily in the temple courts and to break bread from house to house, sharing their meals with gladness and sincerity of heart,
praising God and enjoying the favor of all the people. And the Lord added to their number daily those who were being saved.

pneuma.gloss: Spirit, breath, wind. The word plays on the sound of wind that fills the house as the Holy Spirit is poured out. The Spirit is the promised gift of the Father, given by the exalted Christ, who fills the believers and empowers their witness. Pentecost is the outpouring of the Spirit on the whole people of God.

glossa.gloss: Tongue, language. At Pentecost the disciples speak in other tongues, real human languages, so that the gathered nations each hear the wonders of God in their own speech. The sign reverses the scattering of Babel, gathering the nations to hear one gospel.

christos.gloss: Christ, Messiah, the Anointed One. Peter's sermon climaxes in the declaration that God has made the crucified Jesus both Lord and Christ. He is the promised King from David's line, risen and enthroned, the one on whom the name of the Lord is called for salvation.

metanoeo.gloss: To repent, to change one's mind and turn. Cut to the heart, the crowd asks what to do, and Peter calls them to repent and be baptized in Jesus' name. Repentance is the turning from sin to the crucified and risen Lord that opens the way to forgiveness and the gift of the Spirit.

koinonia.gloss: Fellowship, sharing, common life. The first believers devote themselves to the fellowship, holding things in common and sharing with the needy. It is a partnership created by the Spirit, expressed in teaching, table, prayer, and generous care for one another.

basic.context: This is the day the church is born. Fifty days after Passover, at the feast of Pentecost, the promised Holy Spirit is poured out on the waiting disciples with a sound like wind and tongues like fire. They begin to speak in other languages, and Jews from all over the world, gathered in Jerusalem, hear the wonders of God in their own tongues. Peter explains the event from the Old Testament, preaches Jesus crucified and risen, and calls the crowd to repent. Three thousand believe and are baptized, and Luke describes the shared life of this new community.
basic.hermeneutics: Read the chapter in its four beats: the Spirit comes, the crowd is amazed, Peter preaches, and the church is formed. The central questions are: what does the outpouring of the Spirit mean, how does Peter use the Old Testament to explain it, what is the heart of his gospel message, and what marks the life of a Spirit-filled church? Peter's sermon is the model: it interprets the moment by Scripture and drives to Christ and repentance.
basic.originalLanguages: The pneuma, the Spirit and wind, is poured out, and the disciples speak in other glossa, tongues, real languages heard by the nations. Peter's sermon climaxes in the declaration that God has made Jesus both Lord and christos, Christ. The crowd, cut to the heart, is told to metanoeo, repent, and the new community devotes itself to koinonia, fellowship, a shared common life.
basic.typology: Pentecost reverses Babel: where God once scattered the nations by confusing their languages, He now gathers them to hear one gospel, each in his own tongue. The feast of Pentecost celebrated the harvest and, in Jewish tradition, the giving of the law at Sinai; now the Spirit is given and a great harvest of three thousand is gathered. The fire that rested on the disciples recalls the fire of God's presence.
basic.christ: Peter's whole sermon is about Jesus: certified by God through miracles, delivered up by God's set plan yet crucified by lawless hands, raised from the dead as David foresaw, and exalted to God's right hand, from where He pours out the Spirit. The outpouring is proof that Jesus reigns. The climax is the declaration that God has made this crucified Jesus both Lord and Christ.
basic.god: The Father plans, foreknows, raises, and exalts; the Son is enthroned and pours out the Spirit; the Spirit fills and empowers the church. Salvation is God's work from beginning to end, and the promise of forgiveness and the Spirit is for all whom the Lord our God will call to Himself. God adds to the church daily those who are being saved.
basic.crossReferences: Joel 2 is the prophecy Peter says is being fulfilled. Genesis 11 records the scattering at Babel that Pentecost reverses. Psalm 16 and Psalm 110 supply Peter's proof that the Christ would rise and reign. Acts 2:42-47 becomes the pattern of church life echoed throughout Acts.
basic.oneStory: The exalted Christ pours out the Spirit He promised, and the gospel breaks out to every nation gathered in Jerusalem. The crucified and risen Jesus is proclaimed as Lord and Christ, thousands turn to Him, and a new Spirit-filled people is born. From this fire the mission will spread outward to the ends of the earth.

context.literary: Acts 2 is the pivot of the book's opening. It fulfills the promise of Acts 1 and launches the mission. It unfolds in four scenes: the Spirit's coming, the crowd's bewilderment, Peter's sermon, and the birth of the church, capped by a summary of the believers' shared life that Luke will echo again later. Peter's sermon is a model of apostolic preaching, weaving Joel, Psalm 16, and Psalm 110 around the death, resurrection, and exaltation of Jesus.
context.historical: Pentecost, or the Feast of Weeks, drew Jewish pilgrims to Jerusalem from across the Roman world, which is why so many language groups are present. The list of nations in verses 9 to 11 sketches the known world from east to west. The third hour of the day is nine in the morning, too early for the drunkenness the mockers allege.
context.canonical: Pentecost fulfills the Old Testament promise of the Spirit poured out on all flesh and the New Testament promise of Jesus that His followers would be baptized with the Spirit. It marks the beginning of the last days and the church age. Its reversal of Babel and its gathering of the nations anticipate the worldwide mission and the final gathering of every tongue before the throne.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that the exalted Christ has poured out the promised Spirit, inaugurating the last days and the mission to all nations, and to present Peter's sermon as the model of apostolic gospel preaching that leads to repentance, forgiveness, the gift of the Spirit, and the shared life of the church.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: Luke describes what happened on a unique, foundational day. The abiding truths are that the Spirit is given through the exalted Christ, that the gospel of the crucified and risen Lord calls for repentance and faith, and that the marks of church life, teaching, fellowship, bread, and prayer, are patterns to embrace. The particular phenomena, such as the wind, fire, and the great crowd of nations, describe the founding event and are not commands to reproduce.

debates.question: Is Pentecost a once-for-all founding event, or a repeatable pattern for Spirit-baptism and tongues in every believer's life?

views.label: A unique, unrepeatable founding event
views.case: Pentecost fulfills a specific promise and inaugurates the church age; the tongues are known human languages that gather the nations, and Peter says the gift of the Spirit belongs to all who repent and are baptized, so every believer already receives the Spirit at conversion, Acts 2:38-39. The dramatic signs mark the founding moment.

views.label: A repeatable pattern of Spirit-empowering
views.case: Later outpourings in Acts, such as chapters 8, 10, and 19, and Peter's words that the promise is for all who are far off, suggest to some that a distinct empowering by the Spirit, sometimes with tongues, remains available and is to be sought by believers today.

debates.bearing: All agree the Spirit is given by the exalted Christ and that every Christian receives Him; the difference concerns whether a subsequent, distinct Spirit-baptism with tongues is a normative pattern. Either way the goal is the same: a Spirit-filled people who witness to Christ and call on His name to be saved.

study.originalLanguages: The pneuma, Spirit and wind, fills the house and the believers, who speak in other glossa, languages, so the nations hear the wonders of God. Peter argues from Joel, Psalm 16, and Psalm 110 that the Christ must rise and reign, and concludes that God has made Jesus both Lord and christos, the anointed King. The crowd is called to metanoeo, to repent, receiving forgiveness and the Spirit, and the new community gives itself to koinonia, a fellowship of teaching, table, prayer, and shared goods.

typology.type: The scattering of the nations at Babel, Genesis 11
typology.fulfillment: Pentecost reverses Babel: the Spirit enables the nations to hear one gospel, each in his own tongue, gathering what sin had scattered.

typology.type: The Feast of Weeks and the firstfruits harvest
typology.fulfillment: On the harvest feast the Spirit is poured out and three thousand are gathered, the firstfruits of a worldwide harvest through the gospel.

typology.type: David's throne and the promised Son, Psalm 16 and Psalm 110
typology.fulfillment: David foresaw that his descendant would not see decay but would rise and sit at the Lord's right hand, fulfilled in the risen and exalted Jesus.

study.christ: The chapter is a proclamation of Jesus. He is the man certified by God, delivered up by God's set plan yet crucified by lawless hands, raised because death could not hold Him, and exalted to the right hand of God. From that throne He pours out the Spirit, proving that He reigns. Peter drives to the summit: God has made this crucified Jesus both Lord and Christ, so that everyone who calls on His name will be saved.
study.god: The whole event displays the triune God at work. The Father plans, foreknows, raises, and exalts the Son and gives the promised Spirit. The Son, enthroned, pours out the Spirit. The Spirit fills the church for witness. Salvation is God's initiative, the promise reaching to all whom the Lord our God calls, and it is the Lord who adds daily to the number of the saved.

crossReferences.note: The prophecy Peter cites: God will pour out His Spirit on all flesh, and everyone who calls on the name of the Lord will be saved.
The scattering at Babel through confused languages, reversed at Pentecost as the nations hear one gospel.
David's confidence that God would not let His Holy One see decay, which Peter applies to the resurrection of Christ.
The Lord said to my Lord, sit at My right hand; Peter's proof that Jesus is exalted and enthroned as Lord.

study.oneStory: Pentecost is the outpouring of the Spirit that the prophets promised and Jesus pledged. The exalted Christ sends the Spirit, the gospel breaks out to every nation, thousands repent and believe, and a new Spirit-filled people is born. This is the launch of the mission that will run from Jerusalem to the ends of the earth, gathering a people from every tongue who call on the name of the Lord.

quiz.q: What happened when the Holy Spirit was poured out at Pentecost?

options.text: The disciples were filled with the Spirit and spoke in other tongues, so the gathered nations heard the wonders of God.
options.note: This is exactly what Luke records: the Spirit fills them and they declare God's wonders in many languages.

options.text: A sound like wind and tongues like fire appeared over the disciples.
options.note: True signs of the moment, but the heart of the event is the Spirit filling them so the nations heard the gospel in their own tongues.

options.text: The apostles performed healings to prove they were not drunk.
options.note: The signs of wind, fire, and tongues, not healings, marked Pentecost; Peter answered the charge of drunkenness with Scripture.

options.text: The temple was filled with the glory of God and the priests fell down.
options.note: This does not describe Pentecost; the Spirit fell on the disciples in the house where they were gathered.

quiz.q: How did Peter explain what the crowd was seeing and hearing?

options.text: As the fulfillment of Joel's prophecy that God would pour out His Spirit in the last days.
options.note: Peter says plainly, this is what was spoken by the prophet Joel.

options.text: As a fulfillment of Old Testament prophecy.
options.note: Correct in general, but Peter names the specific prophecy of Joel about the outpouring of the Spirit.

options.text: As a special blessing given only to the apostles for their office.
options.note: Peter says the promise is for the crowd and their children and all who are far off, not the apostles alone.

options.text: As a sign that the law was being given anew from Sinai.
options.note: Peter points to Joel and the Spirit's outpouring, not to a new giving of the law.

quiz.q: What is the climax of Peter's sermon about Jesus?

options.text: God has made this crucified Jesus both Lord and Christ.
options.note: This is the summit of the sermon in verse 36, the risen Jesus enthroned as Lord and Messiah.

options.text: God raised Jesus from the dead because death could not hold Him.
options.note: A true and central claim, but the sermon climaxes further, in the declaration that Jesus is Lord and Christ.

options.text: Jesus was a great teacher approved by God through miracles.
options.note: Peter does say God certified Jesus by miracles, but that is his starting point, not the climax that Jesus is Lord and Christ.

options.text: Jesus will return soon to restore the kingdom to Israel.
options.note: That is not the point of this sermon; Peter proclaims the crucified and risen Jesus as Lord and Christ.

quiz.q: When the crowd was cut to the heart and asked what to do, what did Peter tell them?

options.text: Repent and be baptized in Jesus' name for forgiveness, and receive the gift of the Spirit.
options.note: Peter's answer in verse 38 joins repentance, baptism, forgiveness, and the gift of the Spirit.

options.text: Repent and turn from their sins.
options.note: Repentance is essential and part of the answer, but Peter also called them to baptism and promised forgiveness and the Spirit.

options.text: Keep the law more carefully so they might be forgiven.
options.note: Peter did not point them to law-keeping but to repentance and faith in the crucified and risen Jesus.

options.text: Wait quietly to see whether God would forgive them.
options.note: Peter did not counsel passive waiting; he called for repentance and baptism with the promise of forgiveness now.

quiz.q: What marked the shared life of the first church?

options.text: Devotion to the apostles' teaching, fellowship, the breaking of bread, and prayer, with glad and generous sharing.
options.note: Luke's summary in verses 42 to 47 lists exactly these marks of the new community.

options.text: Meeting together and sharing their possessions with those in need.
options.note: True and part of the picture, but it leaves out their devotion to teaching, breaking of bread, and prayer.

options.text: Withdrawing from the temple and the wider public.
options.note: The opposite is true: they met daily in the temple courts and enjoyed the favor of the people.

options.text: Choosing leaders and building their own place of worship.
options.note: Luke describes teaching, fellowship, bread, and prayer, not building projects or elections.

quiz.q: What does Pentecost show about the exalted Christ?

options.text: Enthroned at God's right hand, He received and poured out the promised Spirit.
options.note: Peter says the Spirit's outpouring is proof that Jesus is exalted and reigning, verse 33.

options.text: He sent the Spirit to comfort the disciples in His absence.
options.note: The Spirit does come from the ascended Christ, but the point is that His reign is proved, not merely that the disciples are comforted.

options.text: He empowered the disciples to speak so people would admire them.
options.note: The Spirit's power was for witness to Christ and the gathering of the nations, not for the disciples' admiration.

options.text: He had not yet been given any authority from the Father.
options.note: The opposite is true: He is exalted to the right hand of God and reigns as Lord and Christ.

questions: The Spirit was poured out so the nations could hear the wonders of God in their own tongues. How does it move you that God reaches across every barrier of language and culture to make Himself known?
Peter preached Jesus crucified, risen, and exalted, and the crowd was cut to the heart. When has the good news of Jesus pierced your own heart and moved you to respond?
Peter called the crowd to repent and turn to Christ. Is there an area of your life where the Spirit is calling you to turn, and what would repentance look like there?
The first believers devoted themselves to teaching, fellowship, bread, and prayer. Which of these four is strongest in your own life, and which most needs to grow?
The early church shared generously with anyone in need. How might the Spirit be prompting you to hold your possessions more loosely for the sake of others?
The Lord added to their number daily those who were being saved. Who in your life might God be drawing to Himself, and how could you be part of that work this week?

translationNotes.note: Pentecost, from the Greek for fiftieth, is the Feast of Weeks or Shavuot, celebrated fifty days after Passover; a footnote links it to the Feast of Harvest in Exodus 23:16 and Exodus 34:22.
The BSB reads Cretans and Arabs, we hear them declaring the wonders of God; the printed text uses a dash before we, which is rendered here as a comma for a clean break.
Peter quotes Joel 2:28-32, following the wording of the Greek Old Testament, applying the promised outpouring of the Spirit to the day of Pentecost.
The promise for you and your children and all who are far off uses a dash in the printed text before to all whom the Lord calls; it is rendered here as a comma for a clean break.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Acts 2:1-47. Footnote markers, line breaks, and poem markers were skipped or treated as spaces when building verse text; the Joel and Psalm quotations were joined from their text elements. Two em dashes in the BSB text, at verse 11 and verse 39, were converted to commas per the no-dash rule and noted in translationNotes. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
